metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the post-migration semantics for `cap_change_events`: either (a) state the mechanism for deriving historical cap changes through the new stack, or (b) explicitly declare it unconditionally Unavailable (cap file gone → no history to mine) and remove the 'reads via analyzer' claim from AC1 for this metric.
evidence: cap_change_events (git_metrics.py:167-229) runs 'git log -p ... -- .github/module-size-limit.txt' to surface old_limit/new_limit pairs from git history
dep 18f9 (scc LOC adapter) is a point-in-time shell-out with no time-series capability
dep e7a0 ([code_health] config) holds a single static size_cap value — no history
AC1: 'cap_change_events reads via the analyzer / config' — no mechanism stated for the historical dimension
ABSENCE: plan body never addresses what cap_change_events should return post-migration when the limit file no longer exists
findings.finding: `cap_change_events` is a git-history reader (git log -p over .github/module-size-limit.txt to find historical cap value changes), but the plan's replacement stack — analyzer (point-in-time scc/lizard invocation) + [code_health] config (static key) — has no historical dimension. The plan says 'cap_change_events reads via the analyzer / config' (AC1) and 're-express' (Review-driven corrections) but provides zero mechanism for how a git log of file changes is re-expressed through a point-in-time tool. As written, the implementing engineer has no specified output for this metric post-migration: suppress it, make it unconditionally Unavailable, or something else.
findings.location: AC1 / Review-driven corrections: 'BOTH limit-file readers must be migrated: module_size_distribution AND cap_change_events'
scenarios: Implementer cannot fulfill AC1 for cap_change_events via analyzer/config because neither has git-history access; either leaves the git log seam intact (violating AC1) or silently drops the metric (unspecified behavior).
If the metric is silently converted to Unavailable, downstream consumers expecting historical cap-change data get no signal, with no stated contract for this case.
findings.suggested_fix: In AC1, split the clause: 'module_size_distribution, oversized_module_count, module_size_trend read via the analyzer / config; cap_change_events is retired to Unavailable (the limit file it tracked no longer exists as a governance artifact; accruing_since preserved).' Remove cap_change_events from the 'reads via analyzer' obligation.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify the data-flow for `module_size_trend`: either name the time-series data source (e.g. snapshot store, repeated scc invocations over git history) that enables a trend value, or explicitly scope it out of this ticket's 'wire to analyzer' AC and leave it as Unavailable pending a future ticket.
evidence: registry.py:135-141 shows module_size_trend currently uses _no_data_yet — a stub
dep 18f9 (scc_loc.py) produces per-file/per-language LOC + max_loc for a single invocation at a single point in time
ABSENCE: no snapshot store, time-series DB, or historical LOC log is referenced anywhere in the plan as the trend data source
'module_size_trend' by its name implies change-over-time; a single LOC value cannot constitute a trend
findings.finding: `module_size_trend` is specified to be wired to the analyzer, but the analyzer (scc via dep 18f9) is a single-point-in-time LOC snapshot — it has no temporal series. A trend metric requires at least two time points. The plan provides no mechanism for how a single-invocation analyzer result produces a trend value, leaving the implementing engineer without a workable data-flow.
findings.location: Review-driven corrections: 'module_size_trend / oversized_module_count are currently SEED stubs … this ticket WIRES them to the analyzer'
scenarios: Implementer runs the scc analyzer, gets a single max_loc number, and has no way to compute a trend — either returns None (Unavailable, same as the stub) or fabricates a scalar as a trend, neither of which fulfills the AC.
If module_size_trend is meant to be deferred (still Unavailable after this ticket), then listing it in AC1 as 'wired to the analyzer' is incorrect.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify the replacement data source and computation protocol for `cap_change_events` after migration: either specify which AnalyzerResult field or context attribute provides cap-change history, or explicitly state the metric becomes permanently Unavailable (with rationale) and update AC#1 accordingly.
evidence: Plan AC: 'cap_change_events read via the analyzer / config; no .github/module-size-limit.txt reference remains'
Current implementation: parses 'git log -p' on .github/module-size-limit.txt to yield [{commit, old_limit, new_limit}] (git_metrics.py:167-229)
3b30 AnalyzerResult fields: loc, complexity, duplication — none represents git commit history of a config file
No dep ticket (18f9, 21fb, 3ba0) provides a git-history AnalyzerResult field
Plan says 're-express' but does not define the replacement computation or whether the metric becomes permanently Unavailable
findings.finding: `cap_change_events` is specified to be 're-expressed via the analyzer / config', but `AnalyzerResult` (defined in 3b30 with fields loc/complexity/duplication) has no field for git-commit history. The plan gives no alternative data source or computation protocol for what `cap_change_events` should return after migration.
findings.location: AC line 1 / Review-driven corrections ('BOTH limit-file readers must be migrated: … cap_change_events') / git_metrics.py:167-229
scenarios: Implementor cannot fulfill AC#1 ('cap_change_events reads via the analyzer') without inventing a new data source not present in any dep ticket.
If the metric is simply dropped or permanently Unavailable, that is a behavioral regression that must be explicit in the plan.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the computation protocol for `module_size_trend` in the plan: specify the input field(s) from `AnalyzerResult`, the output shape (scalar, list, dict), the time window (if any), and the fallback when the analyzer returns None for loc.
evidence: Plan: 'module_size_trend / oversized_module_count are currently SEED stubs (compute=_no_data_yet -> None); this ticket WIRES them to the analyzer (they read nothing today)'
registry.py:135-141: module_size_trend has compute=_no_data_yet — no existing logic to reference
AnalyzerResult.loc (from 3b30) is described as 'per-file/per-language LOC + max' — no trend/series field
No dep ticket defines what 'trend' means computationally in this context
findings.finding: `module_size_trend` is described as a seed stub to be 'wired to the analyzer', but the plan specifies no computation protocol: it is unclear whether this is a point-in-time LOC value, a time-series delta, or a rolling aggregate, and which `AnalyzerResult.loc` subfield(s) supply the input.
findings.location: Review-driven corrections ('module_size_trend … this ticket WIRES them to the analyzer') / registry.py:135-141
scenarios: Implementor produces a scalar (current max_loc) for module_size_trend, but consumers expect a time-series or slope — the metric name implies temporal change.
Without a fallback definition, if AnalyzerResult.loc is None (tool absent) the compute callable has no guidance on whether to return None or raise.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify the fate of `cap_change_events` after the limit-file is dropped: explicitly declare whether it is (a) retired/always-Unavailable (and add a test asserting that), or (b) re-sourced from a specific signal — if (b), name the concrete signal and confirm it is within the analyzer/config abstraction boundary, not a new git-log path.
evidence: cap_change_events current impl: git_metrics.py:167-229 reads `git log -p` over `_LIMIT_FILE` — a purely git-history signal, not a LOC/complexity/duplication signal
3b30 Analyzer protocol fields are: loc, complexity, duplication (per 3b30 AC) — no commit-history field
Plan AC says 'no .github/module-size-limit.txt reference remains' without stating the metric is retired — implies it must still produce a value somehow
Description says 'remove/re-express both' limit-file readers (module_size_distribution AND cap_change_events) but doesn't distinguish 'retire' from 're-source'
findings.finding: The plan requires `cap_change_events` to 'read via the analyzer / config' (AC line 1), but the analyzer protocol (3b30 fields: loc/complexity/duplication) carries no git-commit-history signal, and once `.github/module-size-limit.txt` is dropped there is no file to git-log over. Implementing 'cap_change_events via the analyzer' as written would require either (a) silently making it always-Unavailable (undocumented), or (b) re-expressing it as git-log over a TOML config file — rebuilding git-scanning under a new abstraction. The plan does not resolve which path is intended, creating a risk of over-engineering or silent metric loss.
findings.location: Acceptance criteria, AC line 1: 'cap_change_events read via the analyzer / config'
scenarios: Implementer tries to wire cap_change_events to resolve_analyzer() and finds no applicable field, invents a new git-log path under the analyzer umbrella (NIH / over-engineering)
Implementer silently makes cap_change_events always-Unavailable but AC passes because no limit-file reference remains — regression tests may not catch the silent drop
Implementer attempts to track [code_health].size_cap config changes via git log on pyproject.toml — a new git-parsing surface not justified by the Analyzer abstraction
findings.suggested_fix: If `cap_change_events` has no equivalent signal once the flat cap file is gone, declare it retired in the AC: 'cap_change_events deregistered from REGISTRY and removed; no stub left in place.' This is the simplest path and avoids over-engineering a config-history tracker.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the disposition of `cap_change_events` when the cap source moves from a git-tracked file to a static config key: either (a) retire/unregister the metric and state that explicitly, or (b) define the concrete alternative history source it will read, since the analyzer LOC signal and `[code_health]` config value have no change-history semantics compatible with the existing `git log -p` mechanism.
evidence: Current cap_change_events (git_metrics.py:167-229) parses 'git log -p' over _LIMIT_FILE='.github/module-size-limit.txt' to detect value changes across commits — it is fundamentally a git-history-of-a-file operation.
e7a0 [code_health] config section stores size_cap as a static int|None — it has no time-series or change-history semantics.
Neither the Analyzer protocol (3b30) nor any adapter (18f9, 21fb, 3ba0) exposes a 'cap change history' capability.
AC says 'cap_change_events read via the analyzer / config' — UNVERIFIED: no described mechanism can replicate the git-history read from a static config value.
Evidence class: Contradicted — the config source is incompatible with the existing git-history mechanism without additional design.
findings.finding: The plan asserts cap_change_events will be 're-expressed via the analyzer / config' (AC: 'cap_change_events read via the analyzer / config'), but no feasible mechanism is specified. cap_change_events currently tracks git commits that changed a git-tracked file (.github/module-size-limit.txt). When the cap source moves to a [code_health] config key, there is no analogous git-tracked artifact to diff — the analyzer LOC signal and the current config value carry no historical change record. The plan does not name whether this metric becomes Unavailable permanently, is retired, or uses a different history source.
findings.location: Acceptance criteria — 'cap_change_events read via the analyzer / config'; Description — 'Re-express the module-size cap as an OPTIONAL policy over the analyzer LOC signal'
scenarios: Implementer must decide whether cap_change_events becomes permanently Unavailable (metric is retired in effect), is removed from the registry, or requires a new mechanism not described in the plan — this decision is load-bearing but absent.
If the implementer mistakenly returns an empty list [] instead of Unavailable, the AC's 'no fabricated zeros' criterion is violated silently.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence to the Description or AC: 'cap_change_events is retired (unregistered from REGISTRY) when the limit file is removed; no history-of-config-changes is tracked. The metric returns Unavailable with reason="cap_change_events retired: cap is now a static config policy" when called.' This avoids the capability gap entirely and is consistent with the plan's goal of killing fabricated-zeros mode.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a processing protocol to the plan specifying exactly how `AnalyzerResult.loc` per-file entries map to `{count, near_cap_count, over_cap_count, max_loc}`, including how `size_cap` and `size_near_fraction` from `[code_health]` config are applied to derive the near/over counts.
evidence: Plan AC: 'module_size_distribution … read via the analyzer / config'
Current implementation returns {count, near_cap_count, over_cap_count, max_loc} computed by scanning src/rebar/**/*.py (git_metrics.py:80-115)
3b30 defines AnalyzerResult.loc as per-file/per-language LOC from scc — no 'count', 'near_cap_count', 'over_cap_count' fields exist on AnalyzerResult
e7a0 defines size_cap and size_near_fraction in [code_health] config but the derivation rule mapping those + AnalyzerResult.loc -> the four output keys is nowhere specified
findings.finding: The plan references reading `module_size_distribution` output keys (`count`, `near_cap_count`, `over_cap_count`, `max_loc`) 'via the analyzer' but never specifies the mapping protocol from `AnalyzerResult.loc` (a per-file/per-language scc LOC structure) to those four output keys, nor how `near_cap_count`/`over_cap_count` are computed from raw LOC data against the config `size_cap`.
findings.location: AC line 1 / Description ('read via the analyzer (LOC/complexity/duplication)') / git_metrics.py:80-115
scenarios: Implementor must invent the mapping (e.g. iterate AnalyzerResult.loc per-file entries, apply cap threshold) without a spec, risking divergent behavior across implementations.
If AnalyzerResult.loc returns aggregate max_loc but not per-file breakdown, near_cap_count is uncomputable — the plan gives no fallback.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define 'leaked errno reason' in the plan body — describe the failure mode (e.g., OS file-not-found error surfacing as a metric reason field) and where the guard against it lives.
evidence: 'regression test asserts NO {count:0,max_loc:0} authoritative value and NO leaked errno reason' (AC line 3)
ABSENCE: no mention of errno, OS-level error codes, or error-reason leakage in Description, Review-driven corrections, or Files likely touched sections
findings.finding: AC line 3 requires 'NO leaked errno reason' but no part of the plan body describes what an 'errno reason' is, where it could leak from, or what work prevents it. This is an orphan verification requirement with no corresponding described deliverable.
findings.location: Acceptance criteria line 3
scenarios: An implementer writing the regression test doesn't know what 'leaked errno reason' means in this context — they either skip the assertion or guess incorrectly, leaving the criterion unverifiable.

evidence: 2 criterion line(s) bundle multiple deliverables with 'and' (split so each is independently verifiable).
findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete proving command for AC1, e.g. `grep -rn 'module-size-limit\|\.github.*limit\|src/rebar.*\.py' src/rebar/metrics/git_metrics.py src/rebar/metrics/registry.py` exits non-zero if any reference remains.
evidence: AC1: 'no src/rebar or .github/module-size-limit.txt reference remains in the metric readers'

Verification section only mentions: 'Regression test (foreign repo -> honest Unavailable, not fabricated zeros); full in-repo rebar metrics green for unaffected metrics.' — no grep/search command for reference elimination.
findings.finding: AC1 ('no src/rebar or .github/module-size-limit.txt reference remains in metric readers') has no concrete proving command; the Verification section does not include a grep or search invocation to verify absence.
findings.location: Acceptance criteria AC1 / Verification section
scenarios: A residual import or path string referencing .github/module-size-limit.txt survives in git_metrics.py but tests pass because the code path is never exercised in the test suite.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Verification: `grep -rn 'module-size-limit\.txt\|src/rebar' src/rebar/metrics/git_metrics.py src/rebar/metrics/registry.py && echo FAIL || echo PASS` — the command should fail (no matches) to confirm AC1.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit assertion to the regression test that the `reason` field of the Unavailable response does NOT contain errno strings (e.g. assert `'errno' not in result.reason and 'No such file' not in result.reason`).
evidence: AC3: 'regression test asserts NO {count:0,max_loc:0} authoritative value AND NO leaked errno reason'
Verification: 'Regression test (foreign repo -> honest Unavailable, not fabricated zeros)' — covers the fabricated-zeros side but not errno leakage.
findings.finding: AC3's 'no leaked errno reason' sub-criterion has no associated proving command or test assertion in the Verification section.
findings.location: Acceptance criteria AC3 / Verification section
scenarios: The metric returns Unavailable but with a reason field populated by an internal OS errno string, leaking implementation details; the test suite misses it.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify which closed `source` value (`structural`, `git`, `sidecar`, or `snapshot`) `module_size_trend` and `oversized_module_count` should carry after being wired to the scc analyzer, co-located with the migration spec in the plan.
evidence: registry.py:38-40: SOURCES = frozenset({'structural', 'git', 'sidecar', 'snapshot', 'backfill_classified'})
registry.py:135-149: module_size_trend and oversized_module_count carry source='structural'
18f9 AC: scc_loc.py reads from a deterministic shell-out (scc binary) — semantically closer to 'sidecar' or 'structural' but this is not resolved
Plan text: 'wire … to the analyzer' with no mention of which source label to apply
findings.finding: The `source` enum value for the re-wired `module_size_trend` and `oversized_module_count` metrics is never specified. The closed vocabulary in `registry.py` is `{structural, git, sidecar, snapshot, backfill_classified}`; the seed stubs currently carry `source='structural'`, but post-migration they read from the scc analyzer (an external tool shell-out). The plan does not state which closed `source` value the re-wired specs should use.
findings.location: registry.py:135-149 (seed stubs) / AC line 1 / Description ('wire … onto the analyzer')
scenarios: Implementor assigns source='structural' (carries over from stub) but the actual computation is a shell-out to scc — 'structural' is misleading and may affect is_authoritative() filtering.
Implementor assigns a novel unlisted string, silently breaking is_authoritative() which only passes known values.
findings.suggested_fix: Annotate in the plan that scc-backed metrics should carry `source='sidecar'` (external tool, local shell-out) to match the existing vocabulary intent, consistent with how 18f9 describes the scc shell-out.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `tests/unit/metrics/test_git_metrics.py` and `tests/unit/metrics/test_git_metrics_heldout.py` to 'Files likely touched' and describe what each test needs (rewrite or removal) as `module_size_distribution` and `cap_change_events` are migrated off the limit-file path.
evidence: tests/unit/metrics/test_git_metrics.py:23: `from rebar.metrics.git_metrics import churn, module_size_distribution, refactor_to_addition_ratio`
tests/unit/metrics/test_git_metrics.py:50: `(repo / ".github" / "module-size-limit.txt").write_text(...)` — tests the exact limit-file read being removed
tests/unit/metrics/test_git_metrics_heldout.py:17-18: imports both `cap_change_events` and `module_size_distribution`
tests/unit/metrics/test_git_metrics_heldout.py:98: asserts REGISTRY contains `module_size_distribution` and `cap_change_events` as `source='git'` — this label may change post-migration
findings.finding: The two existing git_metrics unit test files that directly test the functions being migrated (`test_git_metrics.py` imports `module_size_distribution`; `test_git_metrics_heldout.py` imports both `module_size_distribution` and `cap_change_events`) are unenumerated in 'Files likely touched'. Both files assert behavior against `.github/module-size-limit.txt` and `src/rebar/**/*.py` paths that this ticket removes — they will fail or need significant rewriting.
findings.location: Files likely touched (plan section)
scenarios: After migration, `module_size_distribution` no longer reads `.github/module-size-limit.txt` — `test_git_metrics.py:test_module_size_distribution` writes that file and calls the old function signature, causing a test failure.

`test_git_metrics_heldout.py:test_git_metrics_registered_with_labels` asserts `module_size_distribution` and `cap_change_events` have `source='git'`; if they become analyzer-backed (`source='structural'`), this assertion fails.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the exact [code_health] config key name for the module-size cap (e.g. `max_module_loc`) so the executor can implement without consulting e7a0.
evidence: 'Cap value comes from the [code_health] config section created in e7a0 (recorded dep via the adapter chain); consuming that config is THIS tickets job.'
AC2: 'Module-size cap sourced from [code_health] config as an optional policy over the LOC signal (not hardcoded).' — no key name given
findings.finding: The [code_health] config key name for the module-size cap is not stated in the plan; an executor must infer it from ticket e7a0, which is not described here.
findings.location: Description / 'Cap value comes from the [code_health] config section created in e7a0'
scenarios: Executor implements the config lookup under a different key name than e7a0 defined, causing a silent mismatch at runtime.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC2 or Description: 'Key name is `max_module_loc` (or whatever e7a0 defined); if absent/null, no cap is enforced (oversized_module_count -> Unavailable rather than 0).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete end-to-end proving command that exercises the new analyzer path in-repo, e.g. `python -m rebar metrics --metric module_size_distribution` and assert the output contains real LOC data (not Unavailable) when the analyzer is configured.
evidence: Verification: 'full in-repo rebar metrics green for unaffected metrics' — no command given
The plan cuts over module_size_distribution, oversized_module_count, module_size_trend, cap_change_events to the analyzer path; no AC exercises this live path with a named run command.
findings.finding: 'Full in-repo rebar metrics green' is not a concrete proving command and does not demonstrate the new analyzer code path executes end-to-end in production configuration; the cutover from file-readers to analyzer is not validated by a named command.
findings.location: Verification section
scenarios: The test suite runs with a mocked analyzer and passes green; the real analyzer integration is broken but untested.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Verification: `python -m rebar metrics --metric module_size_distribution | jq -e '.value.max_loc > 0'` as the live-path exercise for the in-repo case, and separately `python -m rebar metrics --metric module_size_distribution --repo /tmp/foreign | jq -e '.status == "unavailable"'` for the foreign-repo case.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add 3b30 to the formal Dependencies / `depends_on` list to match the 'analyzer.py is created by 3b30' statement in Review-driven corrections.
evidence: 'analyzer.py is created by 3b30 — reference, do not re-create' (Review-driven corrections)
'Dependencies: 18f9, 21fb, 3ba0 (adapters). Recorded edges: depends_on 18f9, 21fb, 3ba0' (Dependencies section — 3b30 absent)
findings.finding: 3b30 is named in 'Review-driven corrections' as the ticket that creates analyzer.py — making it a hard dependency — but 3b30 does not appear in the formal 'Dependencies' section, which only lists 18f9, 21fb, 3ba0. An implementer following only the Dependencies section would proceed without 3b30 being resolved first.
findings.location: Review-driven corrections section vs. Dependencies section
scenarios: Implementer starts work before 3b30 lands, finds analyzer.py missing, and either re-creates it (violating the constraint) or blocks with no stated dependency to wait on.
findings.suggested_fix: Change the Dependencies line to: 'Dependencies: 18f9, 21fb, 3ba0, 3b30 (adapters + analyzer). Recorded edges: depends_on 18f9, 21fb, 3ba0, 3b30.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rollback/back-out plan (e.g., a feature flag, an expand-contract sequence leaving the old readers in place until the new path is validated in CI, or a stated 'revert commit X' procedure) for the removal of the hardcoded file readers.
evidence: 'no src/rebar or .github/module-size-limit.txt reference remains in the metric readers' (AC line 1 — hard removal, no shim)
'No analyzer configured -> Unavailable' (AC line 3)
ABSENCE: no expand-contract sequence, no feature flag, no rollback instructions anywhere in the plan
findings.finding: The plan removes existing hardcoded readers (module_size_distribution, cap_change_events reading .github/module-size-limit.txt) and replaces them with an analyzer-backed path, but provides no rollback or back-out plan. If the adapter chain (18f9/21fb/3ba0/3b30) is incomplete at deploy time, all four metrics become permanently Unavailable with no stated recovery path short of reverting the entire ticket.
findings.location: Description ('killing the fabricated-zeros mode') and AC line 1
scenarios: Deployed to an environment where the analyzer adapter is misconfigured or a dependency ticket (3ba0, 3b30) is not fully landed — all four module-size metrics go dark with no way to restore the prior behavior without a code revert.
findings.suggested_fix: Introduce an expand-contract phase: keep the old readers behind a config guard (e.g., `analyzer_backed = false` default) in the first deploy, validate the new path in CI, then remove the guard in a follow-up commit — or at minimum document the revert procedure explicitly.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add or update an ADR / AGENTS.md entry capturing the 'analyzer-backed metric readers with honest Unavailable contract' pattern so future metric tickets implement it consistently.
evidence: No mention of ADR / AGENTS.md / design-doc update anywhere in the plan.
The 'optional policy over the LOC signal' and 'honest Unavailable vs fabricated zeros' contract is a new pattern crossing multiple components (git_metrics, analyzer, registry, config adapter chain).
'analyzer.py is created by 3b30 — reference, do not re-create' implies a multi-ticket architectural decision whose contract is never documented in this plan.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a novel architectural pattern — analyzer-backed metric readers with an 'honest Unavailable' contract and optional policy-over-signal design — but does not call for any ADR, AGENTS.md, or design-doc update to capture this decision.
findings.location: Description section; Files likely touched: src/rebar/metrics/analyzer.py (consume), src/rebar/metrics/git_metrics.py
scenarios: A future maintainer adding a new metric reader doesn't know whether to use the analyzer pattern or direct file I/O, because the policy is undocumented.
The Unavailable contract expectation is implied by AC but not formally recorded, leading to inconsistent implementation across future metric tickets.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a brief AGENTS.md section (or ADR stub) under a 'Metric Reader Contract' heading: (1) all code_health readers MUST source data from the analyzer, not filesystem paths; (2) missing/unconfigured analyzer MUST yield Unavailable, never fabricated zeros; (3) optional policy (e.g., cap) MUST come from [code_health] config.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Instrument the 'analyzer unavailable' degradation path with at least a structured log warning (including the specific reason: missing config, missing adapter, etc.) so operators can distinguish intentional unconfigured state from regressions.
evidence: AC states 'NO {count:0,max_loc:0} authoritative value and NO leaked errno reason' but says nothing about emitting a log warning or incrementing a degradation counter.
Files likely touched do not include any logging/observability module.
Absence: no mention of 'log', 'warn', 'trace', 'metric', or 'alert' anywhere in the plan.
findings.finding: The plan specifies 'No analyzer configured → Unavailable' as graceful degradation but does not mention instrumentation (log line, metric counter, or trace) for the analyzer-unavailable path, leaving operators with no signal when degradation silently occurs.
findings.location: Acceptance criteria: 'No analyzer configured -> Unavailable'; Verification section
scenarios: Analyzer becomes misconfigured in production; all code_health metrics silently return Unavailable; no alert fires; operators have no visibility into why the dashboard is empty.
A deployment mistake drops the [code_health] config section; the silent Unavailable is indistinguishable from 'feature not yet configured' vs 'regression'.
findings.suggested_fix: In the analyzer-not-configured branch, emit a structured log at WARNING level: `log.warning('code_health.analyzer unavailable: %s', reason)` before returning Unavailable, where `reason` distinguishes 'no config key' from 'adapter init failure'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify that ticket 3b30 (which creates src/rebar/metrics/analyzer.py, the Analyzer protocol, AnalyzerResult, and resolve_analyzer) is fully merged before this ticket begins implementation — or add an explicit landing-order guard (e.g. import guard / CI pre-check) since the file is currently absent at HEAD.
evidence: Ticket 3b30 status: 'open'; signed manifest entry: 'dep absent src/rebar/metrics/analyzer.py'
Repo search for 'analyzer.py' under src/rebar/metrics returns no matches
Repo search for 'AnalyzerResult' across all of src/rebar returns no matches
Repo search for 'resolve_analyzer' across all of src/rebar returns no matches
Plan phrase: 'analyzer.py is created by 3b30 — reference, do not re-create'
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'analyzer.py is created by 3b30 — reference, do not re-create' as if src/rebar/metrics/analyzer.py already exists, but ticket 3b30 is still open and its own signed verification manifest explicitly records 'dep absent src/rebar/metrics/analyzer.py' at HEAD. The file does not exist in the repo. An implementer following the plan's direction to 'reference, do not re-create' would attempt to import a module that doesn't yet exist, making this ticket un-landable unless 3b30 lands first.
findings.location: Review-driven corrections section: 'analyzer.py is created by 3b30 — reference, do not re-create'
scenarios: Implementer writes 'from rebar.metrics.analyzer import resolve_analyzer' following the plan's instruction to reference (not re-create) the file, but the import fails because 3b30 has not yet landed.

If this ticket is picked up before 3b30 completes (tickets are both open), the AC 'read via the analyzer' cannot be satisfied — the Analyzer protocol, AnalyzerResult, and resolve_analyzer do not exist yet.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Dependencies section: 'BLOCKING: 3b30 must be merged (src/rebar/metrics/analyzer.py present) before this ticket can be implemented.' Also add a smoke-check in the verification step: `python -c 'from rebar.metrics.analyzer import resolve_analyzer'` must pass before wiring the readers.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `3b30` and `e7a0` to the formal `Dependencies` / `depends_on` edge list so the prerequisite is a direct, machine-verifiable edge rather than an inferred transitive one.
evidence: Plan body: 'analyzer.py is created by 3b30 — reference, do not re-create'
Plan body: 'Cap value comes from the [code_health] config section created in e7a0 (recorded dep via the adapter chain)'
18f9 ticket deps include `3b30` and `e7a0` explicitly as `depends_on`
21fb ticket deps include `3b30` and `e7a0` explicitly
3ba0 ticket deps include `3b30` and `e7a0` explicitly
A previous plan-review cache for a related ticket (0734) flagged 3b30 as missing from the formal deps list
findings.finding: Ticket 3b30 (creates `src/rebar/metrics/analyzer.py`) and ticket e7a0 (creates the `[code_health]` config section) are missing from the formal `Dependencies` line. The plan references both ('`analyzer.py` is created by 3b30'; 'Cap value comes from the `[code_health]` config section created in e7a0') but only lists `18f9, 21fb, 3ba0` as deps — these three themselves depend on 3b30 and e7a0, so the prerequisite is only reachable transitively.
findings.location: Dependencies section: 'Dependencies: 18f9, 21fb, 3ba0 (adapters)'
scenarios: If a sequencing tool schedules this ticket before 3b30 or e7a0 complete, `analyzer.py` and `[code_health]` config will not exist yet, making the migration unexecutable even if 18f9/21fb/3ba0 are also not yet done.
The 'recorded dep via the adapter chain' rationale is fragile — transitively implied deps are not machine-verifiable by dep-graph tooling that checks only direct edges.
findings.suggested_fix: Change the Dependencies line to: `Dependencies: 3b30, e7a0, 18f9, 21fb, 3ba0`. Add `depends_on 3b30` and `depends_on e7a0` to Recorded edges.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a brief positive rationale to the Description explaining why the analyzer/config approach is chosen (e.g., language-agnostic LOC measurement via scc, policy/mechanism separation via [code_health] config, testability via the analyzer seam).
evidence: Description states WHAT: 'Wire the code_health metric specs to read via the analyzer … instead of scanning src/rebar/**/*.py and .github/module-size-limit.txt'
ABSENCE: no sentence explaining why the analyzer approach is better — no mention of language-agnosticism, polyglot repos, testability, or configurability as motivating factors
findings.finding: The plan states the chosen approach (migrate to analyzer + config) but provides no positive rationale for WHY this approach fits — no stated benefit such as language-agnosticism, configurable scan roots, testability via the analyzer seam, or separation of policy (cap) from mechanism (LOC measurement). Absence of positive rationale is a G6 criterion gap.
findings.location: Description section
scenarios: An implementer or reviewer cannot verify that the chosen approach is the right one vs. simply changing the hardcoded path to a configurable path, without a stated rationale.
findings.suggested_fix: Prepend to Description: 'Rationale: the analyzer seam (scc via 18f9) gives language-agnostic LOC measurement over any configured scan root, decoupling the metric from the Python-only src/rebar hardcode; the [code_health] config (e7a0) separates the size-cap policy from the measurement mechanism and makes it repo-configurable.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify that a `'metrics'` extra entry is added to `_optional.py`'s `EXTRAS` dict (or that 21fb uses an already-registered extra name) before this ticket wires `oversized_module_count`/`module_size_trend` to the lizard complexity analyzer — otherwise `guard_import(extra='metrics')` raises `ValueError` instead of `OptionalDependencyError`, breaking the Unavailable fallback contract.
evidence: 21fb AC: 'Extra not installed -> `OptionalDependencyError` caught -> Unavailable(reason, accruing_since)'
21fb description: 'lizard is used AS AN IMPORTED LIBRARY here (via `guard_import(extra="metrics")`)'
_optional.py lines 32-45: EXTRAS = {'agents': ..., 'eval': ..., 'tracing': ..., 'grounding': ...} — no 'metrics' key
_optional.py lines 73-74: guard_import raises ValueError for unknown extra names before any ImportError
0734 AC: 'module_size_trend / oversized_module_count ... WIRES them to the analyzer'
findings.finding: Ticket 21fb (complexity adapter, a dep this plan consumes) asserts `guard_import(extra='metrics')` as the absent-lizard guard, but `_optional.py`'s `EXTRAS` dict has no `'metrics'` entry — only 'agents', 'eval', 'tracing', 'grounding'. `guard_import` validates the extra name and raises `ValueError('unknown extra ...')` before the ImportError path is reached, so the 'lizard absent → Unavailable' contract asserted by 21fb is broken. This ticket (0734) wires `oversized_module_count` and `module_size_trend` to the analyzer output, so the broken guard propagates to these metrics' Unavailable path.
findings.location: Dependencies section / ticket 21fb AC ('Extra not installed -> OptionalDependencyError caught -> Unavailable'); src/rebar/_optional.py EXTRAS dict
scenarios: Any environment where lizard is not installed: guard_import(extra='metrics') raises ValueError('unknown extra "metrics"') rather than OptionalDependencyError, so the Unavailable fallback is never reached and the metric crashes instead of degrading gracefully.
In-repo `rebar metrics` with lizard absent: oversized_module_count and module_size_trend produce an unhandled exception rather than Unavailable, breaking AC3.
findings.suggested_fix: Add `'metrics': ('lizard', 'LOC/complexity/duplication analyzers for the code_health lens')` to the `EXTRAS` dict in `src/rebar/_optional.py` as part of 21fb (or as a prerequisite step in this ticket), so `guard_import(extra='metrics')` resolves correctly and missing-lizard maps to `OptionalDependencyError → Unavailable`.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
